claim: Perceptual grouping into unified wholes creates area-based quantity illusions that persist across product contexts and consumer segments.
Source characteristic incongruity increases message involvement and argument scrutiny, reversing traditional credibility-persuasion predictions.

Equal motivation for system defense across confidence levels produces distinct behavioral expressions—low confidence adopts ambiguous consumption choices while high confidence demands explicit commitment.

phenomenon: embodied motor simulation in visual persuasion
claim: Subtle visual cues that facilitate automatic motor simulation of object interaction increase behavioral intentions through grounded cognition mechanisms.
Product placement congruent with learned spatial-temporal mental models enhances processing fluency and valuation, with effects reversed in right-to-left reading cultures.
Differential construal levels between sellers and buyers (abstract vs. concrete) produce asymmetric weighting of high-level vs. low-level features, explaining seller-buyer price discrepancy.
